behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from recommendation results regardless of their relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is purely in the filter predicate — does it check stock status before the relevance score is applied? This is a single function boundary. REAL-INFRA: No I/O needed; a fixed in-memory product list with varied stock flags and scores is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: The filter logic is self-contained; mocking nothing is required. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Considered Integration in case stock status is fetched from a real inventory service, but the behavior says 'marked as out-of-stock' (a field on the product object), so no inter-module fetch is required to catch the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a list of 10 products with mixed stock statuses (some in-stock, some out-of-stock) and varied relevance scores — including an out-of-stock item with the highest score. Pass the list through the stock-filter function and assert the output contains zero out-of-stock products regardless of their scores.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives inside the filter predicate: does it correctly read the user's blocked-category list and compare against a product's category? This is one function's logic. REAL-INFRA: No I/O — a User settings object and a product list are the sole inputs. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking nothing; both inputs are in-process. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Considered Integration if user settings are fetched from a real DB, but the behavior says 'explicitly blocked in their settings,' implying the settings object is already resolved and available to the filter; the inter-module fetch is not part of this behavior's failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user settings object with blocked categories [Electronics, Toys] and a product list spanning Electronics, Toys, Books, and Clothing. Call the category-filter function and assert the returned list contains only Books and Clothing products, with zero Electronics or Toys products.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only surfaces when the browsing-history data module and the ranking/scoring module are wired together — the ranking algorithm must actually receive and act on the browsing signal. A Unit test on either module in isolation cannot catch a mis-wiring. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network required; a local in-memory or local-DB setup is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the ranking function's scoring would trivially pass and hide the failure. LLM-DEP: The algorithm 'weighs' multiple signals — described as a scoring function over inputs, not an opaque ML call. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: The behavior asserts a directional ranking property ('higher than'), which can be verified with a controlled, deterministic input set. No concrete evidence the scoring step is a non-deterministic ML inference call.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the user-profile service with a browsing history in category A and no history in category B. Populate a product set with 5 category-A products and 5 category-B products with equal baseline attributes. Run the full ranking pipeline with the real user-profile and ranking modules wired together, then assert every category-A product's position is above every category-B product's position in the returned list.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure lives in the pagination function itself: does it compute the correct offset, apply a stable sort key, and slice deterministically? All of this can be exercised with a fixed in-memory ranked list. REAL-INFRA: No DB or network required; the ranked list is the sole input. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing needs mocking. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Considered Integration if ordering is delegated to a DB ORDER BY clause (a different failure boundary), but the behavior describes pagination over an already-ranked recommendation list, so ordering stability is tested in the pagination layer itself.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a fixed ranked list of 50 products. Call the pagination function twice with page=2 and page_size=10, with no mutation between calls. Assert both calls return the identical 10 product IDs in the identical sequence, and that those IDs correspond to positions 11–20 in the original ranked list.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears when the user's browsing history module and the serendipity logic inside the recommendation algorithm interact — the serendipity step must read the actual set of browsed categories to determine what is 'unexplored.' Neither module alone surfaces this failure. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network required. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the algorithm would hide whether it actually inspects browsed categories. LLM-DEP: Serendipity injection can be deterministic (always inject top-N items from categories not in browsing history), so no inherent ML non-determinism is required. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: The behavior sets a binary threshold ('at least one'), verifiable with a controlled catalog; does not require observing ML output quality across random runs.
behaviors.test_description: Seed the user-profile service with a browsing history exclusively in categories A and B. Populate the product catalog with products spanning categories A, B, C, and D. Run the recommendation algorithm with real profile and catalog modules wired together. Assert the returned list contains at least one product whose category is neither A nor B.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure occurs at the boundary between the user-profile module (returning an empty history) and the recommendation algorithm (which must detect cold-start and switch to a popularity fallback). Either module alone cannot reveal this handoff bug. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; local popularity data and user profile are sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the algorithm's cold-start branch hides whether it is triggered by the real empty-history signal. LLM-DEP: Popularity ranking is deterministic (sorted by sales count or view count). REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: Correctness here is 'non-empty results ordered by a deterministic popularity metric,' not quality judgment by an ML model.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user with no purchase or browsing history. Populate the product catalog with products having known sales-count values. Wire the user-profile service and recommendation algorithm together with the real cold-start fallback path enabled. Call the recommendation endpoint and assert: (1) the result set is non-empty, and (2) the top result has the highest sales count among all products in the catalog.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure surface spans the click-event handler and the tracking storage module — a bug could exist in the serialization of the position field or in how the timestamp is generated before being written. A Unit test of the handler alone with a mocked store cannot catch schema or serialization mismatches. REAL-INFRA: A local in-process or file-based store is sufficient; no real HTTP/OS required. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the storage write would hide whether the correct fields are actually persisted. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for System: No real network or external service required; a local test store captures the relevant failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Submit a click event specifying product_id='P42', position=3, at a known frozen timestamp. Wire the click-event handler with the real tracking store (local test DB or in-memory store). After the event is processed, query the store and assert the persisted record has exactly product_id='P42', position=3, and a timestamp within 1 second of the frozen value.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Performance cannot be measured without the full stack — query planner behavior, serialization, network stack, and JIT/GC effects all contribute. REAL-INFRA: Requires a real data store with 100,000 real product records and a real HTTP request path. A mocked or in-memory store with synthetic data will not produce representative latencies. MOCK-HIDE: Any performance test with mocked I/O is measuring mock overhead, not system performance — the failure would be completely hidden. LLM-DEP: No. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: Integration with a local lightweight store cannot reproduce realistic query plan behavior or I/O characteristics at 100K-product scale; the failure boundary is defined by real infrastructure under representative load.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a real data store with 100,000 products matching the production schema. Start the recommendation service as a subprocess against this store. Issue a recommendation request for a representative user via real HTTP and record the wall-clock round-trip time from request to complete response. Assert the measured latency is below 200ms. Run the test three times and assert the p95 latency is below 200ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure only appears when the purchase-event handler updates user state AND the recommendation algorithm subsequently reads that updated state to adjust rankings. Neither module in isolation reveals the cross-module state propagation bug. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network required; a local state store is sufficient. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking either the purchase handler's write or the algorithm's read would hide the real inter-module state handoff. LLM-DEP: Deprioritization ('reduce score for purchased products') is a deterministic scoring adjustment, not an ML inference call. REJECTION-GATE for Agentic: The behavior asserts a directional rank change for a specific product (P moves lower), not overall recommendation quality, and can be verified with a controlled input set.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the purchase-event handler, user-state store, and recommendation algorithm together. Retrieve recommendations and record the list position of product P (assert it appears). Record a purchase event for P. Retrieve recommendations again with the same user state and assert P's position is lower than before (or P is absent from the list). Confirm no other product's relative order changed due to the purchase.

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Determinism can only be tested by running the real algorithm twice and comparing outputs. A mocked algorithm is trivially deterministic and hides all real non-determinism. REAL-INFRA: No OS process boundary required. LLM-DEP: The recommendation algorithm uses collaborative filtering ('similar-user behavior') and a serendipity injection mechanism (behavior 5). Both are common sites of stochastic behavior — CF models may use approximate nearest-neighbor search with random tie-breaking; serendipity injectors may sample from unexplored categories. Running the real algorithm 100 times with the same user state could yield different ranked orderings. REJECTION-GATE for Integration: I considered stopping at Integration (wire the real modules and compare two runs). However, I cannot point to concrete text in the behavior description that disqualifies Agentic: there is no statement that the algorithm is deterministic by construction, and the serendipity requirement (behavior 5) is positive evidence of a stochastic component. Absent disqualifying evidence, I keep Agentic. MULTI-STEP: Not required; single pipeline call is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: With a fixed, fully-seeded user state (known purchase history, browsing history, and no pending events), call the full real recommendation pipeline — including the collaborative-filtering scoring model and serendipity injection — twice in immediate succession with no state changes between calls. Assert both calls return exactly the same ordered list of product IDs. Repeat the pair of calls five times to surface intermittent non-determinism.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test description runs the 'full real recommendation pipeline' including the ML scoring model, making this genuinely Agentic rather than Integration — correctness depends on what the real model produces, and the test is designed to detect when it produces different outputs on identical inputs.
